column2: A user moved a desktop to a new desk. The computer powers on, but the monitor shows "No signal." What should you check first before replacing the monitor?
column3: Start with the simplest external display cause.
column4: Confirm that the monitor has power.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

This scenario changed the physical display path, but power is still the first simple external check. A powered-off monitor can make a working computer look like a display failure.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic HighYield Display InputSource Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A monitor remains blank after you confirm power, input selection, brightness, and cable seating. What is a useful next test to narrow the fault?
column3: Use a test that shows whether the problem follows the display path.
column4: Try the monitor with another known-good computer.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

Testing the monitor on a known-good computer checks whether the monitor can display an image outside the original system. That is stronger evidence than guessing which display-path component failed.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic Display KnownGood Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A PC displays firmware startup screens, but the monitor goes blank when Windows loads. What startup mode should you try before replacing the monitor?
column3: Separate pre-OS video from operating-system display configuration.
column4: Use a low-resolution or recovery display mode.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

The monitor has already shown it can display video before the OS loads. That points away from immediate monitor replacement and toward a Windows display configuration or driver path that can be isolated with a basic startup mode.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic HighYield Display Drivers Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A conference-room projector runs for a few minutes, becomes very hot, then shuts down. What projector-specific condition should you inspect before blaming the laptop?
column3: Projectors add heat variables that normal monitors do not.
column4: Inspect projector airflow.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

The symptom follows projector operation time and heat, not the laptop signal. Airflow is a specific, noninvasive check before replacing or troubleshooting the computer.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic HighYield Projector Cooling Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user's external LCD monitor works, but text looks fuzzy after the resolution was changed. What is the best first setting to verify?
column3: Match the system output to what the fixed-pixel display is designed to show.
column4: Verify the computer is using the monitor's native resolution, or a compatible same-aspect setting if native resolution is not available.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

LCDs have a fixed pixel grid. A mismatched resolution can make a working display look poor, so settings should be checked before replacing hardware.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic HighYield Resolution Display Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A lobby display shows a faint outline of the same menu even after the input changes. What should you tell the user and try first if the panel is an LCD?
column3: Distinguish temporary image retention from a cable or input-source problem.
column4: Explain that it may be image sticking and try displaying a plain white screen for an extended period before deciding the panel must be replaced.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

The symptom remains after the content changes, so the input source is less likely. Some LCD image retention can improve, but permanent burn-in or panel damage may still require replacement or escalation.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic BurnIn Display Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A monitor has one tiny spot that is always black, regardless of the app, cable, or computer used. What is the most likely category of issue?
column4: A dead pixel or panel defect, not a cable, power, or operating-system issue.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

The spot stays fixed across inputs and systems. That makes the panel itself the evidence target, while cable and OS theories become less likely.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic DeadPixels Display Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user's screen flashes off and on when the desk is bumped. What should you check first?
column3: Look for the simplest signal-path problem suggested by movement.
column4: Check and reseat the video cable connections, then try a known-good cable if the symptom continues.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

Movement-sensitive flicker strongly suggests an intermittent signal path. Cable checks are faster and less disruptive than driver changes or hardware replacement.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic HighYield Display Cables Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user says the monitor suddenly looks very blue after working normally yesterday. What monitor setting should you check before replacing the display?
column3: Start with the display's own color controls.
column4: Check the monitor's color preset or tint setting.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

An overall color cast can come from the display's own controls. This card tests that one local setting before broader display-path troubleshooting.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic Color Display Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A monitor displays video over HDMI, but the user hears no sound from its built-in speakers. What operating-system setting should you verify before replacing the monitor?
column3: The working video path does not guarantee that audio is routed there.
column4: Confirm that the OS audio output is set to the display connection.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

HDMI, DisplayPort, or Thunderbolt can carry audio, but the operating system may still send sound to a different output device.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic Audio HDMI Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A laptop display is visible only faintly, but an external monitor works normally. What simple setting should you check before escalating for display repair?
column3: Rule out an intentional dim setting before suspecting repair.
column4: Check the laptop brightness setting.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

The external monitor confirms the computer can generate video. Brightness is a specific, reversible check before escalating for panel lighting repair.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic HighYield DimDisplay Backlight Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: An older monitor connection shows a missing color channel after the cable was unplugged and reconnected. What should you inspect?
column3: A physical connector problem can change image quality without changing the app.
column4: Inspect the video cable and connector pins, then reseat or replace the cable if damage is found.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

Missing or damaged pins can affect color or signal quality. The recent cable handling makes connector inspection a better first step than reinstalling drivers.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic Cables Color Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A display shows distorted geometry and intermittent image problems after a new monitor was installed. What OS settings should you verify first?
column3: Make sure the computer output matches what the display supports.
column4: Verify the resolution and refresh rate match the monitor's supported specifications.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

A new monitor changes the supported display modes. Checking resolution and refresh rate is a targeted, reversible test before replacing hardware.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic HighYield Resolution RefreshRate Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: Only one graphics-heavy application shows display corruption. The desktop and other apps look normal. What reversible test can help separate a driver path from display hardware?
column3: Use a software isolation step before treating the monitor as failed.
column4: Disable hardware acceleration for the affected app or test with a basic video driver path, then observe whether the corruption stops.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

If corruption is limited to a hardware-accelerated app, the display panel is less likely than a software or driver path. The test is reversible and gives evidence before replacement.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic Drivers Display Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A projector shows the full desktop, but the image is stretched and does not fit the screen correctly. What should you check?
column3: The display may be resizing the computer output.
column4: Check the display or projector scaling/aspect setting and the computer's display resolution.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

Sizing problems can come from how the output is scaled, not from a broken projector. Match the output and scaling behavior before swapping hardware.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic Scaling Display Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user sees lines, flashing colors, and blocks before the operating system loads. What does that test result suggest?
column3: Use the timing of the symptom to decide whether an application is likely to be the cause.
column4: The issue is less likely to be limited to one application or OS display setting; continue isolating the hardware display path.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

Artifacts that appear before the OS loads point away from an app-only problem. That does not prove which component failed, but it supports hardware-path isolation with cable, display, port, adapter, or graphics hardware tests.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic HighYield Artifacts Display Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A monitor issue disappears when the same monitor is moved to another desk. What does that result help you narrow?
column3: Use where the symptom follows to narrow the failing path.
column4: The original desk's cable, port, adapter, dock, power, or computer path is more likely than the monitor panel itself.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

Known-good movement changes the scope. This Anki card tests one result and what it rules in or out.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic KnownGood Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: You need to escalate a display issue after testing with a known-good cable and a second monitor. What tested-path evidence should your ticket include?
column3: Preserve what you already isolated.
column4: Document which cable, port, and display were tested and the result.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

The next technician needs to know what was already isolated so they do not repeat the same known-good tests.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic HighYield Escalation Documentation Source::Messer-v170

column2: A desktop was moved, the monitor has power, and the monitor reports "No signal." What physical display-path check should you make next?
column3: Movement makes a simple signal-path issue more likely.
column4: Check that the video cable is seated securely.
column5:
Why

A moved desktop can leave the monitor powered but disconnected from video. Cable seating is a specific check before replacing the display.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic Display Cables Source::Messer-v170

column2: A monitor has power and the video cable is seated, but it still reports no signal. What monitor setting should you verify?
column4: Verify that the selected input matches the connected cable.
column5:
Why

A display can be connected correctly but listening on another input. This check is fast, reversible, and specific to display troubleshooting.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic Display Input Source::Messer-v170

column2: A projector overheats during meetings. What maintenance item can restrict airflow and should be checked?
column3: Consider what can restrict cooling.
column4: The projector filter.
column5:
Why

Projector filters and vents can restrict airflow. That makes cleaning or service checks more targeted than blaming the laptop video output.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic Projector Cooling Source::Messer-v170

column2: A projector shuts down after several minutes and its fan continues running after the image turns off. What behavior should you allow before restarting it?
column4: Allow the projector to complete its cooldown period.
column5:
Why

Projectors may need cooldown time to protect the bulb and internal components. Repeated immediate restarts can work against the cooling process.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic Projector Cooling Source::Messer-v170

column2: A monitor's colors look unusually warm or dim late in the day, but the same display looks normal in firmware screens. What OS feature should you check?
column3: Look for a software setting that intentionally changes color output.
column4: Check the operating system's night-light or color mode setting.
column5:
Why

OS color modes can change the display output without a hardware failure. The firmware-screen comparison helps separate software configuration from panel damage.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic Color DisplaySettings Source::Messer-v170

column2: A display is selected as the OS audio output, but the monitor speakers are still silent. What local monitor control should you check?
column4: Check the monitor volume or mute setting.
column5:
Why

The operating system can route audio correctly while the monitor itself is muted or turned down. This keeps the card focused on one local control.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic Audio DisplaySettings Source::Messer-v170

column2: A monitor has built-in speakers, but its manual says audio requires a separate input for the current connection. What should you verify?
column3: Not every display path carries audio in the same way.
column4: Verify that the required separate audio cable or input is connected.
column5:
Why

Some display setups do not use the video cable for audio. Checking the expected audio path avoids replacing a working monitor.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic Audio Cables Source::Messer-v170

column2: A laptop's built-in display is dim only when running on battery. What setting category should you check?
column3: Compare what changes when the power source changes.
column4: Check battery or auto-dimming power settings.
column5:
Why

A symptom tied to battery operation points to power-management settings before panel repair. This is separate from a display that remains faint on all power sources.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic DimDisplay PowerManagement Source::Messer-v170

column2: A laptop image remains very faint after brightness and power settings are verified, and an external monitor works normally. What failure path becomes more likely?
column4: A built-in display backlight or panel lighting failure.
column5:
Why

The external monitor shows video output works. A persistent faint image points toward the display lighting path rather than the whole graphics subsystem.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic HighYield DimDisplay Backlight Source::Messer-v170

column2: A display shows blocks and flashing colors. What known-good test can help decide whether the symptom follows the cable?
column3: Change one part of the signal path at a time.
column4: Test with a known-good video cable.
column5:
Why

A known-good cable is a targeted way to test the signal path without replacing the monitor or graphics adapter first.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic Artifacts KnownGood Source::Messer-v170

column2: Display artifacts remain after a known-good cable is used. What next known-good test can help decide whether the problem follows the monitor?
column4: Test with a known-good display.
column5:
Why

Changing the display after the cable test helps isolate whether the symptom follows the monitor or remains with the computer, adapter, port, or driver path.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic Artifacts KnownGood Source::Messer-v170

column2: Before escalating a display issue, what recent-change detail should you include if the symptom began after a docking or monitor change?
column4: Document the docking or monitor change and when the symptom started.
column5:
Why

Recent changes help the next technician focus on the affected display path. This card keeps the retrieval target to one documentation detail.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic Escalation Documentation Source::Messer-v170

column2: A known-good monitor remains blank when connected to the user's computer with a known-good cable. What does that test result suggest?
column3: Follow the failed test result back to the remaining side of the path.
column4: The problem is more likely on the computer's display output path.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

A known-good monitor and cable reduce suspicion on the display and cable. The remaining likely area is the computer-side output, such as its port, configuration, adapter, or video subsystem.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic Display KnownGood Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user's monitor is blank on their desktop, but the same monitor displays normally on another computer. What does that result rule out first?
column4: It rules out the monitor itself as the most likely fault.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

The symptom did not follow the monitor to another computer. The next theory should focus on the original computer, cable path, input selection, port, or configuration instead of replacing the monitor.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic Display KnownGood Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: Windows loads, but the display is using an unsupported or incorrect mode. What configuration area should you check?
column4: Check the operating-system display settings.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

Display settings can create a symptom after the OS loads even when firmware screens display correctly. This is a configuration check, not proof that the monitor has failed.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic Display Settings Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A display issue started immediately after a video driver update. What driver action is more evidence-based than replacing the monitor?
column3: Use the recent change to choose the next theory.
column4: Roll back or correct the video driver.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

The timing links the symptom to the driver change. That makes the driver path a better next target than replacing a monitor that worked before the update.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.52; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.3
column10: A+::220-1201::5.3 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingDisplayIssues Basic Display Drivers Scenario Source::Messer-v170
